description: Legal definitions extracted from public domain court opinions

Auer deference.definition: The rule that a court must defer to an agency's reasonable interpretation of its own genuinely ambiguous regulation, unless that interpretation is plainly erroneous or inconsistent with the regulation. Also called Seminole Rock deference. Substantially narrowed by Kisor v. Wilkie (2019).
Auer deference.context: Auer deference applies only to an agency's interpretation of its own regulations, not to statutory interpretations — a distinction from Chevron. Kisor (2019) added multiple preconditions before deference is appropriate.

Bivens action.definition: An implied constitutional cause of action for money damages against federal officers who violate an individual's constitutional rights, recognized by courts in the absence of a federal statutory remedy. Available only in limited contexts involving the Fourth Amendment, the Fifth Amendment, and the Eighth Amendment following subsequent restrictions.
Bivens action.case_name: Bivens v. Six Unknown Named Federal Narcotics Agents
Bivens action.context: The Court later substantially limited Bivens actions in Ziglar v. Abbasi (2017), requiring courts to decline to extend Bivens to new contexts absent compelling circumstances.

contribution-expenditure distinction.definition: The constitutional distinction between limits on direct contributions to candidates — permissible as regulations of conduct with only marginal expressive impact — and limits on independent political expenditures — impermissible because they directly restrict the quantity of political expression.
contribution-expenditure distinction.context: Buckley established that the severity of the First Amendment burden, and therefore the level of scrutiny applied, differs depending on whether the regulation targets contributions or independent expenditures.

cell-site location information (CSLI).definition: Time-stamped records generated each time a mobile phone connects to a cell tower, which when aggregated provide a detailed chronicle of an individual's past physical movements.
cell-site location information (CSLI).context: The Court explained that CSLI differs from ordinary business records because individuals do not truly voluntarily share it and it enables near-perfect retrospective surveillance.

Chevron deference.definition: A two-step test for judicial review of agency statutory interpretations: Step 1 asks whether Congress has directly spoken to the question; if the statute is silent or ambiguous, Step 2 asks whether the agency's interpretation is a permissible and reasonable construction of the statute.
Chevron deference.case_name: Chevron U.S.A., Inc. v. Natural Resources Defense Council, Inc.
Chevron deference.context: Established as the controlling framework for agency statutory interpretation from 1984 until overruled by Loper Bright (2024). Courts applied this two-step test to uphold or invalidate agency rules across virtually every area of federal regulatory law.
Chevron deference.superseded_by: Loper Bright Enterprises v. Raimondo, 603 U.S. ___ (2024)

arbitrary and capricious review.definition: Judicial review under 5 U.S.C. § 706(2)(A) requiring a court to engage in a searching and careful inquiry to determine whether an agency considered the relevant factors and avoided a clear error of judgment, without substituting the court's judgment for the agency's on the merits.
arbitrary and capricious review.case_name: Citizens to Preserve Overton Park, Inc. v. Volpe
arbitrary and capricious review.context: Overton Park established that arbitrary-and-capricious review is not a rubber stamp; courts must look hard at the agency's reasoning based on the contemporaneous administrative record, not post-hoc litigation rationalizations.

deliberate indifference (failure to trai.definition: The standard of culpability required to establish municipal liability for failure to train: the municipality must have been on notice that its training was inadequate in a particular area and consciously chose not to correct the deficiency, or the need for training was so obvious that failure to provide it constituted a de facto policy of deliberate indifference.
deliberate indifference (failure to trai.context: Canton's deliberate indifference standard applies to the threshold question of whether a failure-to-train claim can constitute the 'official policy or custom' required for Monell liability.

pre-compliance review.definition: An opportunity for the party subject to an administrative inspection demand to seek judicial review of the demand before being required to comply, thereby preserving Fourth Amendment protections against unreasonable searches.
pre-compliance review.case_name: City of Los Angeles v. Patel
pre-compliance review.context: The Court held that administrative subpoenas and inspection regimes must afford a meaningful opportunity for pre-compliance judicial review to satisfy the Fourth Amendment.

pretermination hearing.definition: The minimum pre-deprivation procedural protection required before a government employer may terminate a classified civil servant: oral or written notice of the charges, an explanation of the employer's evidence, and a meaningful opportunity for the employee to respond before the termination takes effect.
pretermination hearing.case_name: Cleveland Board of Education v. Loudermill
pretermination hearing.context: Loudermill held that this threshold hearing, even if informal, is constitutionally required before property interests in continued public employment may be terminated.

curtilage.definition: The area immediately surrounding and associated with a home that shares the Fourth Amendment protections afforded to the home itself; typically includes attached garages, driveways, and enclosed yards immediately adjacent to the dwelling.
curtilage.context: The Court reaffirmed that the curtilage is constitutionally equivalent to the home for Fourth Amendment purposes, rejecting any automobile exception that would override this protection.

public record privilege (press).definition: The First Amendment protection shielding the press — and the public — from civil or criminal liability for truthfully publishing information that was lawfully obtained from official records open to public inspection, absent a countervailing state interest of the highest order.
public record privilege (press).context: Cox Broadcasting established that once information appears in official public records it loses its private character for First Amendment purposes, and the press may publish it without fear of state-imposed liability.

deliberate indifference (Spending Clause.definition: The standard for imposing liability on a federal-fund recipient for peer harassment under Title IX: the recipient must have had actual knowledge of the harassment and responded — or failed to respond — in a manner that was clearly unreasonable under the known circumstances, effectively ratifying the violation.
deliberate indifference (Spending Clause.case_name: Davis v. Monroe County Board of Education
deliberate indifference (Spending Clause.context: Davis extended the deliberate indifference standard from employee-on-student harassment (Gebser v. Lago Vista) to peer harassment, demonstrating that Spending Clause recipients bear ongoing compliance obligations proportionate to their actual knowledge.

nondelegation doctrine (modern state).definition: The constitutional principle that Congress may not transfer its legislative power to executive agencies without providing an intelligible principle to guide that power. As of Gundy (2019), the Court applies a permissive intelligible-principle test but a fragmented Court signaled potential for tightening; the major questions doctrine in West Virginia v. EPA (2022) now imposes additional constraints for major policy questions.
nondelegation doctrine (modern state).context: Gundy's fractured outcome — five votes to uphold the delegation but four justices ready to revisit the doctrine — created significant uncertainty about the future of the non-delegation doctrine that was partially resolved in West Virginia v. EPA.

qualified immunity.definition: A defense available to government officials sued in their individual capacities for civil rights violations, shielding them from damages liability unless their conduct violated clearly established law that a reasonable official would have known. The inquiry is entirely objective — the official's personal beliefs or intentions are irrelevant.
qualified immunity.context: Harlow reformulated qualified immunity to protect all but the plainly incompetent or those who knowingly violate the law, replacing the prior Scheuer v. Rhodes test that included a subjective good-faith inquiry.

fair warning standard.definition: The principle that officials lose qualified immunity when existing legal standards gave them fair warning that their specific conduct was unconstitutional, even absent a prior case with materially identical facts. Unconstitutionality may be apparent from general doctrinal principles.
fair warning standard.context: Hope v. Pelzer rejected an overly narrow reading of the 'clearly established' requirement that would demand a case with precisely identical facts before officers are on notice that their conduct violates the Constitution.

reasonable expectation of privacy.definition: A two-part standard: the individual must have exhibited a subjective expectation of privacy, and that expectation must be one that society is prepared to recognize as reasonable.
reasonable expectation of privacy.context: Justice Harlan's concurrence articulated this test, which became the governing standard for determining whether the Fourth Amendment is triggered.

genuine ambiguity (regulatory).definition: A precondition for Auer deference: a regulation is genuinely ambiguous only after a court has exhausted all the standard tools of textual interpretation — including text, structure, history, and purpose — and still cannot resolve its meaning. Facial ambiguity is insufficient.
genuine ambiguity (regulatory).context: Kisor held that genuine ambiguity is the threshold inquiry for Auer deference; if standard interpretive tools resolve the regulatory meaning, no deference is owed regardless of the agency's position.

independent judgment.definition: The APA-required standard for judicial review of agency statutory interpretation, under which courts must determine the best meaning of a statute using traditional tools of construction without deferring to the agency's preferred reading simply because the text is ambiguous.
independent judgment.context: Post-Loper Bright, courts no longer apply Chevron deference to agency statutory interpretations; instead, they exercise independent judgment as to what the statute means.

exclusionary rule.definition: The constitutional principle that evidence obtained through a violation of the Fourth Amendment is inadmissible in a criminal prosecution, whether in federal or state court.
exclusionary rule.context: The Court extended the federal exclusionary rule from Weeks v. United States to all state criminal proceedings, holding it to be a constitutionally mandated remedy rather than a discretionary deterrent.

Mathews balancing test.definition: The three-factor test for determining what process the Due Process Clause requires: (1) the private interest affected; (2) the risk of erroneous deprivation through current procedures and the value of additional safeguards; and (3) the government's interest, including the burden of providing greater procedural protections.
Mathews balancing test.context: Established as the controlling standard for procedural due process analysis outside the public employment and welfare benefit contexts that had previously required full hearings.

intelligible principle (applied).definition: A sufficient legislative direction to an agency or commission when Congress specifies the goals, factors, and ranges that must guide the exercise of delegated authority. The intelligible principle standard is satisfied when Congress has made the basic policy choice and left the delegee to fill in the details within those parameters.
intelligible principle (applied).context: Mistretta held that the Sentencing Reform Act's detailed direction to the Commission — specifying seven factors, three purposes, and a mid-range guideline requirement — provided an intelligible principle that satisfies the non-delegation doctrine.

official policy or custom.definition: A municipality's formal enactments, regulations, or decisions adopted through official channels, or persistent widespread practices that, even if not formally authorized, are so well settled as to constitute a custom with the force of law and fairly attributable to the governmental entity.
official policy or custom.case_name: Monell v. Department of Social Services of the City of New York
official policy or custom.context: Monell requires plaintiffs to identify an 'official policy or custom' as the moving force behind the constitutional violation because municipalities are not vicariously liable for the independent acts of their employees.

under color of state law.definition: Acting under the appearance or pretense of state authority: a state official acts under color of law when, while purporting to exercise official duties or powers, the official misuses or abuses that authority — even if the specific conduct violates state law or exceeds the official's actual authority.
under color of state law.context: Monroe established that 'under color of law' is satisfied whenever the official's conduct is made possible by virtue of state-conferred authority, regardless of whether the specific act was authorized or lawful under state law.

hard look review.definition: A rigorous application of arbitrary-and-capricious review requiring courts to verify that the agency examined the relevant data, articulated a satisfactory explanation for its action, considered significant alternatives, and did not overlook an important aspect of the problem.
hard look review.case_name: Motor Vehicle Manufacturers Ass'n v. State Farm Mutual Automobile Insurance Co.
hard look review.context: State Farm crystallized what became known as 'hard look' review, which requires agencies to explain their reasoning with specificity and ensures that important alternatives are genuinely considered before a rule is adopted or rescinded.

freedom of association.definition: A First Amendment right, implicit in the rights of assembly and petition, protecting individuals' ability to associate with others to advance shared beliefs and causes without government compulsion to disclose membership, particularly when disclosure would expose members to retaliation.
freedom of association.case_name: NAACP v. Alabama ex rel. Patterson
freedom of association.context: The Court recognized freedom of association as a constitutional right distinct from—though related to—freedom of speech and assembly, applying it to shield organizational membership lists from compelled government disclosure.

actual malice.definition: The constitutional standard for defamation liability in suits brought by public officials or public figures: the plaintiff must prove by clear and convincing evidence that the defendant made the statement with knowledge of its falsity or with reckless disregard for whether it was false or not.
actual malice.case_name: New York Times Co. v. Sullivan
actual malice.context: Established to prevent defamation suits from chilling vigorous criticism of public officials; prevents officials from using libel law to suppress press coverage of their exercise of official duties.

Spending Clause coercion.definition: The constitutional doctrine that conditions on federal grants are invalid when the financial inducement to comply is so overwhelming that states lose their genuine choice to accept or reject the condition. A condition is coercive when it threatens the loss of pre-existing funds to pressure states into accepting a new, substantially expanded program.
Spending Clause coercion.case_name: National Federation of Independent Business v. Sebelius
Spending Clause coercion.context: First time the Court applied Dole's anti-coercion principle to actually invalidate a federal spending condition; the Medicaid expansion threatened the loss of all existing Medicaid funding — approximately 10% of states' budgets.

municipal immunity (absence of).definition: The rule that municipalities and local government units have no good-faith or qualified immunity defense available in § 1983 suits for constitutional violations caused by their official policies or customs. Unlike individual officers, municipalities bear strict liability when an official policy is the moving force behind a constitutional deprivation.
municipal immunity (absence of).case_name: Owen v. City of Independence, Missouri
municipal immunity (absence of).context: Owen held that § 1983's twin goals of deterrence and compensation require full municipal accountability, without the safe harbor of good faith that individual officers enjoy through qualified immunity.

intelligible principle.definition: The constitutional minimum required for a valid delegation of legislative authority from Congress to the executive: Congress must lay down a discernible policy, standard, or rule that directs the exercise of delegated power, so that the delegation does not amount to a transfer of legislative power itself. First articulated in J.W. Hampton, Jr. & Co. v. United States (1928); applied to invalidate a delegation in Panama Refining.
intelligible principle.context: The statute at issue in Panama Refining contained 'literally no guidance' — no policy, standard, or definition of the circumstances warranting exercise of the delegated authority — making it the paradigm case of an unconstitutional delegation.

clear notice rule (Spending Clause).definition: The requirement that conditions Congress attaches to federal grants must be expressed in clear and unambiguous statutory language, because Spending Clause legislation is contractual in nature and states must have unambiguous notice of what obligations they accept when they take federal funds.
clear notice rule (Spending Clause).case_name: Pennhurst State School & Hospital v. Halderman
clear notice rule (Spending Clause).context: Pennhurst's clear-notice rule ensures that states can make an informed choice about accepting federal funds, since the Spending Clause's legitimacy rests on voluntary acceptance of conditions rather than sovereign compulsion.

experience and logic test.definition: The two-part First Amendment access test: first, whether the proceeding or record has historically been open to the press and general public ('experience'); second, whether public access plays a significant positive role in the functioning of the process ('logic'). Both prongs must be satisfied for a First Amendment right of access to attach.
experience and logic test.context: Adopted in Press-Enterprise II to determine which governmental proceedings — beyond criminal trials — carry a constitutional right of public access that can override closure requests.

right of access (First Amendment).definition: A First Amendment right of the public and press to attend and observe criminal proceedings, derived from historical tradition and the structural role openness plays in ensuring governmental accountability. Closure is permissible only where there is an overriding interest and the closure is no broader than necessary.
right of access (First Amendment).context: Richmond Newspapers was the Court's first explicit recognition of an affirmative First Amendment access right rather than merely a protection against prior restraints on publication.

fungibility rationale.definition: The constitutional justification for federal anti-bribery jurisdiction over officials of federal grant recipients: because money is fungible, corruption of any official of a grant recipient poses a threat to federal funds even if the bribe does not directly involve federally appropriated money.
fungibility rationale.context: Sabri's fungibility rationale underpins 18 U.S.C. § 666 jurisdiction over bribery of state and local officials whose agencies receive federal grants — a critical statutory enforcement tool for federal oversight of grant recipients.

Dole test.definition: The four-part standard for valid Spending Clause conditions: (1) expenditure must serve the general welfare; (2) conditions must be stated unambiguously; (3) conditions must be reasonably related to the federal interest in the program; and (4) conditions must not require violation of independent constitutional prohibitions.
Dole test.context: Dole's four-part test controls whether Congress may attach conditions to federal grants to states; subsequent decisions added a fifth implicit element — the conditions must not be so coercive as to effectively compel compliance.

major questions doctrine.definition: A canon of statutory interpretation holding that when an agency claims authority to regulate on a question of major economic or political significance, courts require a clear statement from Congress — not merely a plausible textual argument — before recognizing that authority. Ambiguous or broadly worded provisions are insufficient to grant transformative regulatory power.
major questions doctrine.case_name: West Virginia v. Environmental Protection Agency
major questions doctrine.context: West Virginia v. EPA crystallized the major questions doctrine as a distinct clear-statement rule that operates independently of the Chevron framework, requiring explicit congressional authorization before agencies may claim authority over questions of vast significance.

Auer deference.definition: The rule that a court must defer to an agency's reasonable interpretation of its own genuinely ambiguous regulation, unless that interpretation is plainly erroneous or inconsistent with the regulation. Also called Seminole Rock deference. Substantially narrowed by Kisor v. Wilkie (2019).
Auer deference.context: Auer deference applies only to an agency's interpretation of its own regulations, not to statutory interpretations — a distinction from Chevron. Kisor (2019) added multiple preconditions before deference is appropriate.

Bivens action.definition: An implied constitutional cause of action for money damages against federal officers who violate an individual's constitutional rights, recognized by courts in the absence of a federal statutory remedy. Available only in limited contexts involving the Fourth Amendment, the Fifth Amendment, and the Eighth Amendment following subsequent restrictions.
Bivens action.case_name: Bivens v. Six Unknown Named Federal Narcotics Agents
Bivens action.context: The Court later substantially limited Bivens actions in Ziglar v. Abbasi (2017), requiring courts to decline to extend Bivens to new contexts absent compelling circumstances.

contribution-expenditure distinction.context: Buckley established that the severity of the First Amendment burden, and therefore the level of scrutiny applied, differs depending on whether the regulation targets contributions or independent expenditures.

cell-site location information (CSLI).context: The Court explained that CSLI differs from ordinary business records because individuals do not truly voluntarily share it and it enables near-perfect retrospective surveillance.

fungibility rationale.context: Sabri's fungibility rationale underpins 18 U.S.C. § 666 jurisdiction over bribery of state and local officials whose agencies receive federal grants — a critical statutory enforcement tool for federal oversight of grant recipients.

Dole test.definition: The four-part standard for valid Spending Clause conditions: (1) expenditure must serve the general welfare; (2) conditions must be stated unambiguously; (3) conditions must be reasonably related to the federal interest in the program; and (4) conditions must not require violation of independent constitutional prohibitions.
Dole test.context: Dole's four-part test controls whether Congress may attach conditions to federal grants to states; subsequent decisions added a fifth implicit element — the conditions must not be so coercive as to effectively compel compliance.

major questions doctrine.definition: A canon of statutory interpretation holding that when an agency claims authority to regulate on a question of major economic or political significance, courts require a clear statement from Congress — not merely a plausible textual argument — before recognizing that authority. Ambiguous or broadly worded provisions are insufficient to grant transformative regulatory power.
major questions doctrine.case_name: West Virginia v. Environmental Protection Agency
major questions doctrine.context: West Virginia v. EPA crystallized the major questions doctrine as a distinct clear-statement rule that operates independently of the Chevron framework, requiring explicit congressional authorization before agencies may claim authority over questions of vast significance.
